Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually carving out time in the evenings after work and on weekends. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found that I needed to actually do things—practice problems, take handwritten notes, physically work through concepts—rather than just reading or watching. I'd set up about 12 hours across the week, but honestly, it wasn't always perfectly organized. I juggled a lot with limited resources, so sometimes I'd study at the library to save on electricity at home or use campus computers when I could.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to be consistent, but with seven online courses running simultaneously, deadlines would pile up and throw everything off. Some weeks I'd be super focused on one course, then have to shift gears completely. The anxiety of keeping track of everything made me feel like I was always slightly behind, even when I wasn't.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or announcements that got me to log in. With so many courses, I was always worried I'd miss something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to calm my nerves—to make sure there wasn't a deadline I'd forgotten about. The discussion forums also pulled me in because I knew participation mattered, and I genuinely found some of the conversations helpful.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, checking the LMS became almost automatic—like checking my phone first thing in the morning. I set specific times to log in, usually morning and evening, which helped manage my anxiety about missing things. It became part of my daily rhythm, especially since I was taking so many online courses. The mindfulness exercises I started doing actually helped me approach it more calmly rather than frantically.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful—I watched a lot of them, over 230 minutes worth. I liked being able to pause and rewind when I needed to process something. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing other perspectives helped concepts click for me. Anything hands-on or interactive felt more engaging than just reading text documents.
Q6: I'll be honest, some of the supplementary readings I barely touched. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades. Some PDFs I'd download with good intentions but never get around to reading thoroughly. If something felt too theoretical without practical application, I'd skim it at best. I know that probably hurt my assignment scores.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to assessments or real-world application. I'm very hands-on, so if I could practice something or apply it immediately, I was all in. Discussion posts felt worthwhile because they helped me process ideas out loud, so to speak. If something felt like busy work with no clear purpose, my motivation—which is already pretty low—would tank completely.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. Required ones had this weight of obligation that pushed through my low motivation. Optional ones? I'd look at them and think 'that would be nice' but rarely follow through unless I was genuinely curious. With everything else on my plate and my anxiety already high, adding optional tasks felt overwhelming. I had to be strategic about where I put my limited energy.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them offline because my internet access isn't always reliable, and I can't afford to waste data. I'd print things at the library and study from paper, take handwritten notes, and work through problems physically. I also used free YouTube videos and external resources when the course materials weren't clicking. A lot of my real learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: So many times! I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then work through my handwritten notes for hours afterward. Or I'd download a problem set and practice it repeatedly on paper—none of that shows up anywhere. My study group met in person at the library, and we'd work through concepts together without any of us being logged in. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study time.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it, and then that material would be my companion for days. I'd review it on the bus, at work during breaks, wherever. It was more practical given my situation—I couldn't always be online, and honestly, I retained information better when I could physically interact with printed materials or my own notes.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when multiple deadlines collided. My anxiety would spike, and even though I have pretty high resilience, I'd feel paralyzed sometimes. Financial stress was always in the background—worrying about bills made it hard to focus on coursework. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through those moments, but it didn't make the underlying challenges disappear.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by life stuff, I'd log into the LMS but just... stare at it. I'd click around without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities were constant—I'm 25, I have real-world obligations that don't pause for coursework. Sometimes the LMS became this thing I'd check compulsively when anxious, but that wasn't productive engagement.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely—worrying about money or my next shift. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing them. My body was present, logged in, but my brain had checked out. Those moments usually happened when my anxiety was highest and I was just going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments a lot, which is why my completion rate isn't great. The anxiety around starting something imperfect was real—I'd rather delay than submit mediocre work. Sometimes I'd avoid discussions because I didn't feel like I had anything smart to contribute. I knew these things were important, but knowing and doing are different beasts when your motivation is low and your anxiety is high.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, my low motivation meant things would drift indefinitely. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage intensely. It wasn't healthy—lots of last-minute cramming and stress—but it worked well enough to get me through. The LMS deadline reminders were actually really helpful for keeping me on track.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Critical feedback sometimes sent my anxiety spiraling, making me avoid that area for a while before I could face it again. I appreciated detailed feedback because it gave me something concrete to work on, but the emotional processing took time. My resilience helped me bounce back eventually.
Q18: The course structure really mattered. Clear modules with defined expectations helped me plan my limited study time effectively. When things were organized logically, I felt less anxious and more capable of tackling the work. Having seven courses meant I needed each one to be predictable so I could mentally map out my week. Surprise changes or unclear instructions threw me off significantly.
Q19: Having videos I could download and watch offline was huge—that accommodated my resource limitations. Discussion forums being asynchronous helped because I could participate when I had time and mental space. What made it harder was when activities required constant internet access or real-time participation. Also, some interfaces were clunky, which wasted precious time I didn't have.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking around, watching videos without really absorbing them, but never quite getting to the submission stage. It could also be someone who's genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets their own standards. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion wasn't great. A lot of my learning happened offline—studying downloaded materials, working with notes, discussing with peers in person. Someone could be deeply engaged with the content in ways the LMS never sees. The platform captures clicks, not comprehension.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how long you spend on videos, whether you participate in discussions. That's useful data. But it misses the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the anxiety behind compulsive checking, the real understanding that happens away from the screen. It's like measuring someone's fitness by how often they enter the gym, not what they do inside.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections to real life. For me, it's physical—writing notes, working through problems with my hands, discussing ideas out loud. It's mental presence, not just digital presence. True engagement survives even when the LMS is closed.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels, which heavily influenced how I interacted with everything. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline with downloaded materials or handwritten notes. It wouldn't show the mindfulness practices that helped me manage my stress enough to stay in the course. And it definitely wouldn't show how hard I worked to stay resilient despite limited resources and low motivation. My engagement story is so much bigger than my login data.
